mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S‑Class petrol engine typically costs £3,000–£7,500 supply only depending on the engine type. A head gasket replacement on the M104 costs approximately £1,500–£2,500. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine you're asking about. The M104 (S280/S320) is a 'fantastic engine; smooth, durable, reliable, and very long‑lived', but head gasket failure is the defining weakness. The M112 (S350) is 'almost indestructible' and 'one of the most reliable Mercedes engines'. The M113 (S420) is 'about the most reliable engine MB made', with one owner reporting 466,000 km. Regular maintenance is essential for all.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M104 head gasket failure (S280/S320), M104 fan bracket bearing failure and rear main seal leaks (S280/S320), M112 oil leaks from breather and valve covers (S350), M113 timing chain tensioner wear and oil leaks (S420), and W220 platform issues (early 2000–2002 cars). See the Era Map above for generation‑specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept W140 S320 (values £5,000–£15,000+ depending on condition), a £3,500–£4,500 reconditioned engine is 25–60% of the vehicle's value — often worth it for a solid example. Against a W220 S350/S420 (£3,000–£10,000), a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 40–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a good example. The Mercedes scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W140 S280 (1993–1998) = M104.944 (2.8L, 193 bhp); W140 S320 (1991–1998) = M104 (3.2L, 231 bhp); W220 S350 (2003–2005) = M112 (3.7L, 245 bhp); W220 S420 (1999–2005) = M113 (4.3L, 279 bhp). The W140 S320 was originally badged as 300SE/SEL before the naming change. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M104 head gasket, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A head gasket replacement costs £1,500–£2,500. The M104's head gasket is a routine maintenance item on higher‑mileage engines due to the aluminium head and cast‑iron block expanding at different rates. If the engine has overheated severely, the head may be cracked or warped, requiring a full engine replacement (£3,000–£6,000). At the first sign of oil leaks or coolant loss, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Budget priority; finding a good donor M104/M112/M113 is becoming harder
These engines are no longer produced new; reconditioned units are the standard

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the W140 and W220 petrol era (1993–2005). Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800 for standard engines. A Mercedes head gasket replacement on the M104 can cost a couple of thousand pounds. Buyers should allocate around £2,500 for a new head gasket on the M104.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an S‑Class petrol engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £85–£130/hr (independent) or £140–£200/hr (London/SE dealer). The M104's inline‑six is reasonably accessible, while the M112 and M113 V configurations add some complexity. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare S‑Class petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M104 Head Gasket Failure (S280, S320 — W140)
problems.symptoms: External oil leak at the front of the engine where the timing chain cover meets the head, oil pooling on the garage floor, oil burning on the exhaust manifold causing smoke at start‑up, coolant loss, overheating, oil in coolant or coolant in oil.
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000–200,000 miles — can occur at any mileage but becomes more common with age

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice oil leaks, coolant loss or overheating, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M104's head gasket is the defining weakness of this otherwise legendary engine. The aluminium head and cast‑iron block expand at different rates under heat cycling, making the head gasket the sacrificial item. This is not a design failure but a consequence of physics — however, it means that head gasket replacement is a routine maintenance item on higher‑mileage M104 engines. The leak is typically external, with oil seeping from the front timing cover area. In severe cases, oil can pool on the garage floor or burn on the exhaust manifold. At the first sign of oil leaks, have the head gasket inspected. Allow around £2,500 for replacement. Some owners recommend letting the car warm up for a few minutes before driving to extend head gasket life.

problems.title: M104 Fan Bracket Bearing Failure (S280, S320 — W140)
problems.symptoms: Unusual noises from the front of the engine, fan touching the water pump, overheating, loud grinding or squealing.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if unusual noises occur

problems.recommendation: The fan bracket bearing on the M104 can fail, typically around 250,000 km. When it fails, the fan can touch the water pump, causing further damage. If you hear unusual noises from the front of the engine, have the fan bracket bearing inspected immediately. Replacement is relatively straightforward but should not be delayed.

problems.title: M104 Rear Main Seal Leak (S280, S320 — W140)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, oil around the transmission bellhousing, burning oil smell, gradual oil loss.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (rear main seal replacement — transmission removal required)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless oil starvation leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; monitor oil levels regularly

problems.recommendation: Rear main seal oil leaks are very common on the M104. They typically present as a gradual slow leak that doesn't get progressively worse, rather than a sudden failure. However, if left unchecked, oil loss can lead to engine damage. Monitor oil levels regularly and have the seal inspected if you notice oil spots.

problems.title: M112 Oil Leaks (S350 — W220)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage from the top of the engine, oil in spark plug wells.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (breather cover and valve cover gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: The M112 V6 is considered one of Mercedes' most reliable engines, but it does suffer from oil leaks. The most common areas are the breather covers on the top of the engine (also known as cambox covers), valve covers, and the oil filter housing. The breather cover oil leaks can cause oil to puddle in the spark plug wells and drip onto the hot manifold, creating smoke. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the system inspected promptly. Resealing the breather covers is a tedious but straightforward job using high‑temperature automotive sealant.

problems.title: M113 Timing Chain Tensioner Wear (S420 — W220)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic clattering from the front of the engine, engine warning light, loss of power, rough running.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if timing chain skips and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M113 V8 is one of Mercedes' most reliable engines — "about the most reliable engine MB made" — but the timing chain tensioner can fail with age. A rattling noise on cold start is the primary symptom, caused by the spring that keeps tension until oil pressure builds up failing. The tensioner itself is a combination of a hollow bolt with a piston insert, a spring and a ratchet that maintains piston position in the absence of oil pressure. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the tensioner inspected. Replacement is not a difficult job.

problems.title: M113 Oil Leaks (S420 — W220)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, visible oil seepage from valve covers, oil cooler seals or front crankshaft seal.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless oil starvation leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are a common concern for the M113 engine, often stemming from worn gaskets or seals. The most frequently affected areas include valve cover gaskets, oil filter housing gaskets, and the front crankshaft seal. The M113 V8 is known for developing slow leaks over time. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the system inspected promptly. Regular seal replacements are part of the cost of running an M113‑equipped vehicle.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes S‑Class S280 / S320 / S350 / S420 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance on higher‑mileage M104s; catch it before head damage
Straightforward replacement; catch it before further damage
Labour‑intensive but essential; catch it before oil starvation
Catch it early — far cheaper than engine damage
These engines are robust; catastrophic failures are rare

buyingChecks.title: Buying This S‑Class Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using Mercedes‑approved oil
* M104 (S280/S320): head gasket replaced or inspected (allow £2,500 for this work if not done)
* M104 (S280/S320): fan bracket bearing in good condition
* M112 (S350): no oil leaks from breather covers or valve covers
* M113 (S420): timing chain tensioner replaced or functioning correctly
* W220 (2003+) facelift cars are more reliable than early (2000–2002) cars
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these engines are durable but unforgiving of neglect
* M104 with significant oil leaks — head gasket work is imminent
* M104 with overheating history — potential head damage
* M112 with extensive oil leaks — multiple gaskets may need replacement
* M113 with cold‑start rattle — timing chain tensioner work is imminent
* Early W220 (2000–2002) cars — the platform is considered one of the worst ever built
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes S‑Class S280, S320, S350 and S420 petrol represent a golden era of Mercedes engineering — over‑engineered, built to last, and powered by some of the most celebrated engines ever made. The M104 straight‑six is smooth and durable but demands head gasket vigilance. The M112 V6 is "almost indestructible", while the M113 V8 is "about the most reliable engine MB made". The W140 is a classic — over‑engineered and built to last, but with complex electronics and air suspension that can be expensive to maintain. The W220 is more modern but early cars (2000–2002) are considered problematic. Buy on service history, prioritise facelift W220 cars (2003+) or well‑maintained W140s, and budget for preventative maintenance. As one owner put it: "The M113 AMG 5.5 is about the most reliable engine MB made. Unfortunately the W220 (especially 2000-02) platform is one of the worst ever built".

cta.label: → Compare S‑Class petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes S‑Class S280, S320, S350 or S420 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * M104 Head Gasket (S280/S320) — Any oil leaks from the front of the engine or oil pooling? Any coolant loss? (allow £2,500 for this work)
* M104 Fan Bracket (S280/S320) — Any unusual noises from the front of the engine?
* M104 Oil Level (S280/S320) — Check dipstick; any oil loss indicates rear main seal or head gasket issues
* M112 Oil Leaks (S350) — Check for oil around the top of the engine (breather covers, valve covers)
* M113 Timing Chain (S420) — Any cold‑start rattle? Timing chain tensioner may be failing
* M113 Oil Leaks (S420) — Check for oil around valve covers and oil filter housing
* W220 Platform — Early (2000–2002) cars have more issues than facelift (2003+) cars
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Head gasket, timing, or oil leak codes are most common

If the engine has overheated severely, the head may be cracked or warped, requiring a full engine replacement (£3,000–£6,000). items.label: SECTION 9, META AND SCHEMA
items.value: Section 9 (Trust + Final CTA), the Meta tags, Open Graph, Twitter Card, Schema markup, and the Production Note.

items.label: The user's deep need here is to get the complete, ready-to-publish variant page content. They're likely using this as part of a larger content generation system, possibly for a website about engine replacements for these premium brands. They need consistency with the established template and all the technical SEO elements included.
items.value: I need to follow the same pattern from all the previous Part 2 responses. That means:

items.label: 1. Write Section 9 with the correct H2 and three trust points tailored to the specific engines in this group (M104, M112, M113). Use the brand-appropriate language (Trust points for Mercedes).
items.value: 2. Create the Meta Title and Description with the right character limits and specific details.

items.label: 3. Generate the Open Graph and Twitter Card tags.
items.value: 4. Write the full JSON-LD Schema. It needs to include all the required blocks: BreadcrumbList, ItemList for the Era Map, ItemList for Engine Codes, FAQPage with the 6 questions from Part 1, Organization, Service, Dataset, and WebPage. I must ensure the engine codes in the schema match the Era Map exactly.

items.label: 5. Write the Production Note, confirming things like aggregate rating omission, the price range source (full Era Map), organization type, dates, and importantly, a line confirming schema/content consistency.
items.value: The structure needs to mirror the previous complete Part 2 examples. I'll also need to carry forward all the specific technical specifications for the engines from Part 1 into the schema where relevant.
